name: Muon g-2 anomaly persistence
summary: The combined BNL+Fermilab world-average muon anomalous magnetic moment a_mu(exp) = 116,592,059(22) x 10^-11 sits ~5 sigma above the Muon g-2 Theory Initiative 2020 Standard Model prediction (data-driven HVP). Lattice QCD predictions for the hadronic vacuum polarisation (BMW 2020 and follow-ups) sit much higher than the data-driven HVP, in tension with the latter at ~4 sigma. Whether the anomaly is real new physics or an artifact of the data-driven HVP determination is unresolved, and constitutes one of the most consequential data tensions in particle physics.

characteristic_numbers.note: Fermilab+BNL 2023 world average, 0.19 ppm precision. Updated Run 2/3 result reaches 0.20 ppm precision per experiment.
5.1 sigma deviation from Muon g-2 Theory Initiative 2020 prediction.
Only ~1 sigma deviation if lattice HVP is correct - shifts the puzzle from 'new physics' to 'which HVP is right'.

principles_stressed.note: If real new physics, points to scalar leptoquarks, vector-like leptons, or specific 2HDM/MSSM scenarios contributing at a_mu ~ 250e-11 level. Many such models are tightly constrained by EW precision and LHC. Resolving lattice-vs-data-driven HVP tension is now the dominant question.

history: BNL E821 (2006): a_mu measured to 0.54 ppm, ~3 sigma above SM. Fermilab g-2 Run 1 (2021, arXiv:2104.03281): confirmed BNL value, world average 4.2 sigma. Fermilab Run 2/3 (2023, arXiv:2308.06230): improved to 0.20 ppm, 5+ sigma vs WP2020. Final Run 1+2+3 combined result (2025) reaches 0.13 ppm. BMW lattice 2020 (arXiv:2002.12347) created the lattice-vs-data-driven HVP problem.

references.citation: Fermilab Muon g-2 Collaboration, 'Measurement of the positive muon anomalous magnetic moment to 0.20 ppm', Phys. Rev. Lett. 131, 161802 (2023)
